coredata.dc:title: Selective photosynthetically active nanozymes alleviate crops cold stress via exclusively enhancing root proliferation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Ding, Zixuan
Xu, Wenlong
Feng, Yingchen
Li, Yuan
Ma, Xiaochi

Shah, Anis Ali

Ren, Xiaoqian
Liang, Jinhua
Huang, Yanan
Xiao, Qingbo
Zhang, Zhiyang
coredata.dc:description: Root proliferation is in high demand to agricultural development, especially to the overwintering crops, the exclusive growth of root is beneficial to alleviate cold damage. Herein, the unique photosynthetically active nanozymes with bright yellow light and catalase-like activity is proposed to selectively promote crops’ root growth. As a demo, we adopted the yellow light carbon dots (YCDs) nanozyme to enhance root proliferation of kale-type overwintering oilseed rape under cold stress (10°C) by seed dressing. The supplemental yellow light transferred from YCDs nanozyme stimulated the up-regulation of relative amino acids related genes and biosynthesis, further facilitate root elongation. The excellent catalase-like activity of YCDs nanozyme could also effectively reduce the reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels into homeostasis. As a result, the total root length and root tips of the oilseed rape increased by 124.95 % and 36.15 %, respectively, surpassing existing plant growth regulators including wood vinegar, melatonin, and even carbon dots (CDs). The exclusive enhancement of root proliferation by the selective photosynthetically active nanozymes offers a promising feasible approach for adapting crops against cold stress, significantly contribute to sustainable agriculture under the unexpected global climate change nowadays.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanozymes
Yellow light
Root proliferation
Cold stress
Oilseed rape

The authors would like to thank Weiwei Wang from Shi yan jia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of YCDs nanozyme. (a) X-ray diffraction patterns, standard PDF card of graphite was inserted, (b) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images and the corresponding particle sizes distribution, (c) Fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths and (inset) optical images under 365 nm excitation; Confocal imaging shows the distribution of YCDs nanozyme in (d) leaves and (e) root of the rice, (f) CAT enzyme-like activity reflected by the rate of oxygen release during H2O2 decomposition, inset shows the optical images of the oxygen bubbles, (g) Steady state kinetic assay with H2O2 as substrate, Km is the Michaelis-Menten constant; Vmax is the maximal reaction velocity; Kcat is the catalytic rate constant.

(a) Apparent morphologys of oilseed rape and microscopic images of root for CK, CDs and YCDs, (b) Root volume, (c) Root length and root tips, (d) Root projected area and root surface area and (e) increased proportion of root volume, (f) increased proportion of root length and root tips, (g) increased proportion of root projected area and root surface area.

(a) Apparent morphologys and (b) root growth modelling, (c) Fresh weight and (d) its increased propotion of roots and shoots, (e) Dry weight and (f) its increased propotion of roots and shoots, (g) The summarized results of established regulators for root growth.

Metabolite profile changes in oilseed rape. (a) Orthogonal partial least squares discrimination analysis ((O)PLS-DA) score plots of metabolic profiles without (CK) and with YCDs treatment (YCDs). (b) Volcano plot of 77 differentially regulated (p < 0.05) metabolites in oilseed rape treated by YCDs. Red and green dots represent up-regulated (65) and down-regulated metabolites (12), respectively. (c) KEGG pathway of oilseed rape after YCDs treatment. (d) Schematic diagram of proposed metabolic pathways in oilseed rape by YCDs treatment. Red and green represent up and down regulated metabolites, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Transcriptome profile changes in oilseed rape. (A) Orthogonal partial least squares discrimination analysis ((O)PLS-DA) score plots of metabolic profiles without (CK) and with YCDs treatment (YCDs). (B) Volcano plot of differentially regulated (p < 0.05) genes in oilseed rape treated by YCDs. Red and green dots represent up-regulated (1378) and down-regulated genes (1841), respectively. (c) Total KEGG pathway enrichment analysis of DEGs treated by YCDs. (d) down-regulated KEGG, (d-I) ∼ (d-VI) MAPK signaling pathway plant, plant hormone signal transduction, starch and sucrose metabolism, phenylalanine metabolism, alpha-linolenic acid metabolism, glutathione metabolism. (e) up-regulated KEGG, (e-I) ∼ (e-VI) heat mapping of fatty acid elongation, zeatin biosynthesis, cutin, suberine and wax biosynthesis, tryptophan metabolism, biosynthesis of unsaturated fatty acids, brassinosteroid biosynthesis. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Oxidation and antioxidant products of roots and shoots. (a) Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), (b) Hydroxyl radicals (·OH), (c) Malondialdehyde (MDA), (d) Glutathione (GSH). Data shown are mean ± SE of 3 replicates. letters on vertical bars represent significant differences between groups according to Duncan's multiple range test (P < 0.05); (e) DAB staining (for hydrogen peroxide, dark brown spots) and (f) NBT staining (for superoxide anion, blue spots) of leaves from cold-stressed rapeseeds treated with CDs and YCDs. Confocal fluorescence imaging of (g) NBT staining and (h) DAB staining. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
YCDs nanozyme could protect oilseed from cold damage due to selective strengthening root growth by yellow light emission and eliminating excessive ROS by CAT-like enzyme.

head.ce:title: Selective photosynthetically active nanozymes alleviate crops cold stress via exclusively enhancing root proliferation

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this research.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A yellow light emitting and catalase like nanozymes was developed to alleviate cold stress of overwintering crops, which could exclusively promote root prefoliation and simultaneously restore ROS homeostasis.

Root proliferation is in high demand to agricultural development, especially to the overwintering crops, the exclusive growth of root is beneficial to alleviate cold damage. Herein, the unique photosynthetically active nanozymes with bright yellow light and catalase-like activity is proposed to selectively promote crops’ root growth. As a demo, we adopted the yellow light carbon dots (YCDs) nanozyme to enhance root proliferation of kale-type overwintering oilseed rape under cold stress (10°C) by seed dressing. The supplemental yellow light transferred from YCDs nanozyme stimulated the up-regulation of relative amino acids related genes and biosynthesis, further facilitate root elongation. The excellent catalase-like activity of YCDs nanozyme could also effectively reduce the reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels into homeostasis. As a result, the total root length and root tips of the oilseed rape increased by 124.95 % and 36.15 %, respectively, surpassing existing plant growth regulators including wood vinegar, melatonin, and even carbon dots (CDs). The exclusive enhancement of root proliferation by the selective photosynthetically active nanozymes offers a promising feasible approach for adapting crops against cold stress, significantly contribute to sustainable agriculture under the unexpected global climate change nowadays.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Under a changing climate nowadays, the agriculture crops are encountering with versatile abiotic stresses such as cold, drought, salinity, etc., which has been the serious threaten to food security globally [1–4]. Overaccumulation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) is a predominant response due to the partial reduction of plant absorbed oxygens in adversities, serving as the primary factor to ROS-mediated programed cell death in crops growth [5,6]. Especially, plants are fragile and most susceptible to the abiotic stresses in the germination and seedling stages [7,8]. With diverse concerns, roots play an essential role in the survival and development of crops, and are also able to trigger specific signaling pathways to adjust their developmental program to adapt different stresses [9–11]. The root system interacts with its environment by modifying its structural feature. Thus, the root proliferation and activity of the seedlings would be suppressed primarily from the overaccumulation of ROS under abiotic stresses [12]. Specifically, for overwintering crops such as rapeseed and wheat, the roots and the shoot apical meristem are the only living organ during prolonged cold exposure, the excessive growth of overground part could result in more vulnerable to cold stress [13]. Therefore, to some extent, besides eliminating excessive ROS, selective enhancing root proliferation at the seedling stage is highly desired to protect, and to maintain its productivity in overwintering crops [14–16].

According to previous reports [16,17], small molecules stimulators such as wood vinegar have been extensively applied to the crops to alleviate abiotic stresses [17], but indiscriminately stimulating the growth of both above-ground organs and roots. There is still lack of an effective strategy to simultaneously strengthen root proliferation and reduce over accumulated ROS of the crop seedlings under abiotic stress. As a comparison, the reasonable utilization of light to be an alternative stimulator should be concerned as the presence of multiple photoreceptors [18–20]. Generally, light energy drives the synthesis of carbohydrates and generation of oxygen from carbon dioxide and water, while photosynthetic response to different light environments should also be concerned. Supplemental green light can facilitate whole-plant growth including overground part and root simultaneously [21]. In contrast, Liu et al reported that yellow light by light emitting diodes (LEDs) can selectively stimulate root growth and activity of the rapeseed while have negligible effect on dry matter accumulation of shoots [22]. Therefore, for overwintering crops under chilly cold stress, the selective root proliferation with the application of yellow light is beneficial to crops survival. However, considering the widespread supplementation of yellow light by LEDs for farmland in the rural area is rather challenging and high cost [23], more feasible and cost-effective technique should be developed to afford yellow light for the crop seedlings under abiotic stresses.

Recently, nanozymes as a kind of novel materials with specific enzyme-like activities have attracted rising attentions in alleviating abiotic stresses of crops with low dosage and high efficiency [24,25]. By precisely designing, some nanozymes such as carbon dots (CDs) or CeO2 can also convert the ultraviolet (UV) light of the solar spectrum into specific light [26]. Inspired by these, we hypothesize the nanozymes emitting yellow light under solar irradiation can serve as a selective photosynthetically active nano stimulator to simultaneously trigger root growth while reduce ROS under abiotic stress. Herein, as a proof-of-concept demonstration, we adopted catalase (CAT)-like carbon dot nanozyme emitting bright yellow light (YCDs) under UV irradiation to selectively enhance root proliferation of the kale-type overwintering rapeseed (oilseed rape). In this research, under cold stress (10 °C), the root proliferation and growth of the oilseed rape are significantly promoted by the supplement yellow light of YCDs successfully. The orthogonal physiological and omics analysis clearly revealed that the enhanced root proliferation by yellow light is achieved through up-regulated genes relative to amino acid metabolism and the enriched expression of root growth-promoting amino acids. Likewise, the reduction of over-accumulated ROS by CAT-like enzyme activity of the YCDs is also demonstrated. These results illustrated that nanozyme-based photosynthetically stimulation strategy could sustainably promote root proliferation and enhance stress resistance of the crop seedlings.

YCDs nanozyme presents yellow light emission and CAT-like activity. Carbon dots (CDs) with various surface states could emit excitation-dependent light [27]. YCDs nanozymes displays two peaks centered at 26.5° and 42.3° in XRD patterns (Fig. 1
a), respectively. This is attributed to the typical characteristic graphitic structure (referenced to XRD standard PDF card of graphite inserted in Fig. 1 a) of the carbon dots. When it comes to the fluorescence emission measurement, bright yellow emission can be observed upon UV irradiation at 365 nm for the YCDs solution (300 mg/L) in the insert of Fig. 1 c. The emission peaks of the YCDs are centered at around 580 nm and keep stable with changing excitation wavelength, confirming the superior capability of converting UV irradiation into yellow light (Fig. 1 c). The nitrogen- and oxygen-rich functional groups such as −C=O and −C=N analyzed bellowing in YCDs nanozyme, provided a lot of sp2-carbon domains in the structure [28], attributing to a yellow light emission. With the supplementary of confocal microscopes (Fig. 1 d and 1e), bright yellow emission both from the leaves and root cells after uptake of YCDs via seed dressing can be observed. In sharp contrast, only red-light emission from chlorophyll in leaves and no emission from roots can be found in the CK (CK represent the oilseed rape seeds without YCDs treatment). This indicates easy penetration into crops cells due to the well dispersibility and small size of the YCDs. Verified by HR-TEM, YCDs were uniformly distributed with an average particle size of 2.3 nm, it would facilitate their penetration into the rapeseed cells. Besides, distinct lattice fringes with space of 0.2 nm are shown (inset in Fig. 1 b), corresponding to the (100) interplanar spacing of graphite carbon.

Besides, the CAT-like activity of the YCDs were also assessed in H2O2 solutions. By adding a specific concentration of H2O2 in PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) to the YCDs solution (300 mg/L), obvious oxygen bubbles are shown within minutes and the number of bubbles gradually increase over time (Fig. 1 f). It reflects fast decomposition of H2O2 into H2O and O2 by the YCDs. The CAT-like catalytic performance of YCDs were further evaluated by the steady-state kinetic analysis, yielding a Km value of 20.05 mM due to a high affinity toward H2O2 (Fig. 1 g). Moreover, the YCDs displayed a maximum reaction rate (Vmax) of 3.54 μM/s and a high catalytic constant (Kcat) of 1.96 s−1, clearly reflecting their excellent CAT-like catalytic activity. To clarify the origin of supreme CAT-like activity, the details of YCDs were further analyzed by FTIR. As presented in Fig. S1 , characteristic peaks at 3450 cm−1 (O–H), 1701 cm−1 (C=O), and 1397 cm−1 (C=N) can be observed, attributing to stretching vibrations of O–H, C=O, and C=N groups, respectively, indicating the hydrophilic property that enables excellent dispersibility of the YCDs and representative molecular structure of YCDs is shown on Fig. S2 . Besides, the higher zeta potential of YCDs dispersed on water (−15.6 eV) in Fig. S3 additionally explain the well-hydrophilic of YCDs. Further, this hydrophilic nitrogen- and oxygen-rich functional groups were also confirmed by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis ( Fig. S4 ). The XPS spectra shows the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen elements on YCDs surfaces, with C=C bonds at 284.3 eV, C-O/C–C bonds at 286.2 eV, and C=N/C=O bonds at 288.1 eV, respectively. Nitrogen spectra reflect the presence of pyrrole N at 399.5 eV and N–H at 401.2 eV, respectively, while O1s spectra show peaks at 531.5 eV (C=O) and 532.9 eV (C-O), respectively. As reported by Gao et al[29]., the presence of hydroxyl, carboxyl groups could act as the substrate-binding sites and the carbonyl groups coupled with π-system, serving as the active catalytic sites, thus facilitating the formation of catalase (CAT)-like activity of the YCDs.

YCDs nanozyme selective enhances root proliferation under cold stress. To investigate the root proliferation effect of YCDs, rapeseeds were selected to be treated with YCDs nanozyme as well as the common blue-emitting CDs ( Fig. S5 ) by seed dressing, respectively. Cold stress (10 °C) is malignant to root growth of seedlings, however, with different treatments, the damage could be alleviated to some extent. As shown in Fig. 2
a, the root lengths of rapeseeds seedlings are much improved and the total root system is more extensive and fibrous treating with YCDs compared to CK and CDs counterpart. Besides, as shown in Fig. 2 b–g, the total root volume reached 0.0177 cm3 after treated with YCDs, ∼121.25 % larger than the blank sample (0.008 cm3) and much higher than the common blue-emitting CDs (0.0105 cm3). Moreover, the total root length can be increased significantly by 124.95 % accompanied with enhanced root tips after YCDs treatment, whereas the root length was only increased by 36.15 % after seed dressing by CDs. Compared to the common CDs counterpart, the root projected area and surface area were also significantly enlarged by the YCDs nanozymes. The robust root system enhanced by YCDs is pivotal in safeguarding oilseed rape from cold damage, facilitating better anchorage in soil and bolstering resistance to cold stress during overwintering.

The capability of selective enhancing root proliferation of the YCDs nanozymes was further investigated by analyzing the growth parameters of roots and shoots in oilseed rape seedlings. The phenotypic characteristics of oilseed rape including dry and fresh weight were evaluated after 27 days of chilling. As illustrated by the morphological changes depicted in Fig. 3
a, YCDs nanozymes could significantly improve oilseed rape under a cold environment. The model showcasing the selective strengthening of root growth by YCDs is presented in Fig. 3 b. Especially, Fig. S6 demonstrates that YCDs treatment led to a notable increase in total fresh weight from 0.097 g to 0.132 g, while CDs application had only a marginal effect on fresh weight (0.096 g). Moreover, both CDs and YCDs seed dressing resulted in increased total dry weight, while it could range from 0.0096 g of CDs treatment to 0.01524 g of YCDs counterpart, highlighting a better beneficial impact of YCDs on oilseed rape under cold stress.

As depicted in Fig. 3 c–d, the fresh weight of the root part reached 0.06 g after YCDs treatment, showing a significantly > 75 % increment compared to the CK and common CDs counterparts. In sharp contrast, the fresh weight of the shoot part was only increased by ∼ 16 % after treating with YCDs, highlighting the selective enhancement of root growth by YCDs. Similarly, in Fig. 3 e–f, the dry weight results for roots and shoots shows a significantly 108.89 % increase with YCDs treatment compared to CK in roots, alongside a less substantial enhancement in shoots dry weight (33.33 %). Unlike YCDs treatment, CDs led to a weaker increment in roots (12.5 %) compared to shoots (16.67 %). The above analysis also shows the common CDs can only enhance growth of both the root and shoot parts without selectivity. Similar phenomenon is also reached in the existing plant regulators such as wood vinegar and melatonin [30,31]. The summarized results of established regulators for root growth are presented in Fig. 3 g [32]. Thus, a potential “selective photosynthetic activation” is achieved with the addition of yellow light emitting materials, only root growth is exclusively enhanced by treating with YCDs nanozyme due to the auxiliary yellow light emission under solar irradiation.

Selective root prefoliation pathways by YCDs nanozyme. To elucidate the mechanisms by which YCDs nanozymes enhance root proliferation and plant growth, a metabolomics analysis was conducted on oilseed rape. The LC-MS-based metabolomics identified and quantified 1,557 metabolites, and orthogonal partial least squares discriminant analysis ((O)PLS-DA) was performed to assess the metabolic changes induced by YCDs treatment. The (O)PLS-DA score plot revealed a distinct separation between YCDs-treated and control samples along principal component 1 (PC1), which explained 31.54 % of the total variance (Fig. 4
a), underscoring the significant impact of YCDs on oilseed rape metabolism. Subsequent one-way ANOVA identified 77 differentially expressed metabolites with a significance threshold of p < 0.05 (Fig. 4 b). Among these, 65 metabolites were up-regulated, and 12 were down-regulated ( Fig. S7 ). KEGG enrichment analysis further elucidated the biological roles of these metabolites. Fig. 4 c illustrates the enriched pathways, with Cluster Profiler identifying the top 20 significantly altered pathways, predominantly related to amino acid and organic acid metabolism (e.g., 2-oxocarboxylic acid metabolism) and synthesis pathways (e.g., protein digestion and absorption, biosynthesis of amino acids, aminoacyl tRNA biosynthesis). Additionally, glucosinolate biosynthesis was significantly changed. Notably, nine metabolites responsible for promoting rapeseed root growth were identified within these pathways based on their significant differential expression characteristics, highlighting their importance in regulating rapeseed growth. In addition, the metabolomics analysis was also conducted on oilseed rape by CDs treatment ( Fig. S8 ) and the role of CDs for oilseed rape growth is different from YCDs treatment. CDs nanomaterials significantly increased the biosynthesis of organic acid and derivatives in oilseed rape, especially the pyruvic acid, succinic acid, proline and betaine.

In detail, several amino acids, including aspartate, isoleucine, leucine, tyrosine, and phenylalanine, increased by 45 % to 130 % under YCDs treatment (Fig. 4 d). Amino acids play crucial roles in modulating plant physiological processes, including serving as osmolytes, regulating ion transport, modulating stomatal opening, and acting as hormones to promote root growth. Aspartate, isoleucine, leucine, and tyrosine are key regulators of nitrogen assimilation and are involved in fundamental processes essential for plant life. The observed changes in these amino acid levels suggest a significant impact on primary nitrogen metabolism. Specifically, these amino acids are critical for promoting cell division and synthesizing elongation factors, thereby inducing root cell division and elongation. Besides, phenylalanine, a precursor for the synthesis of plant hormones such as indole-3-acetic acid (IAA), plays a vital role in root development by stimulating root branching and elongation [33].

In addition to the amino acids associated with enhanced root proliferation, other metabolites such as furoate and ribose were also up-regulated. Furoate acts as a natural plant growth regulator, promoting root growth and development. Ribose, a key component of intracellular energy metabolism, contributes to the synthesis of ATP (adenosine triphosphate), which supports rapid root growth and differentiation. The increased ribose levels also indicate a reallocation within the carbon pool. Conversely, levels of flavonoids (e.g., rutin) and palmitate were decreased under YCDs treatment. On the contrary, rutin may disrupt hormonal balance in plants, affecting auxin synthesis or metabolism and leading to stunted root growth. Similarly, palmitate may impact lipid metabolism, thereby inhibiting root growth or proliferation.

To gain a comprehensive understanding of the altered molecular networks and to elucidate the mechanisms by which YCDs enhance root growth under cold stress conditions, RNA-seq-based transcriptome profiling was conducted. Analysis using the (O)PLS-DA model revealed a distinct separation between the YCDs-treated group and the control (CK) group along COMP1 (Fig. 5
a), indicating significant global transcriptional reprogramming induced by YCDs in oilseed rape. The transcriptomic analysis of oilseed rape identified a total of 52,878 proteins, with 3,219 genes showing differential expression following YCDs treatment. Specifically, 1,378 genes were upregulated and 1,841 genes were downregulated (fold change > 1.2 or < 0.87; p ≤ 0.05) as compared to the CK (Fig. 5 b).

The KEGG pathway enrichment analysis of differentially expressed genes (DEGs) treated with YCDs is presented in the Fig. 5c, reflecting significant changes in gene pathways. Fig. 5 d and 5e illustrates the up-regulated and down-regulated KEGG pathways, respectively, to provide a clear understanding of the transcriptional processes. Additionally, genes classified by pathway in the KEGG enrichment results are analyzed using heat maps, shown in Figures dⅠ∼dⅥ and eⅠ∼eⅥ. The KEGG pathway analyses (YCDs vs. CK, top 20) reveal significant down-regulation in plant hormone signal transduction and MAPK signaling pathways following YCDs treatment.

MAPK, a serine/threonine protein kinase, plays a crucial role in various plant cell signaling pathways by transmitting signals from the cell surface to the nucleus. It is activated by diverse extracellular stimuli, including hormones and cellular stress [34]. Reactive oxygen species (ROS), generated under stress conditions, act as signaling molecules that activate the MAPK pathway, which is essential for plant responses to environmental changes. In rapeseed, ROS levels increase under cold stress, but YCDs mitigate this effect through catalase-like enzyme activity, thereby reducing MAPK signaling. Similarly, plant hormone signal transduction, vital for responding to environmental stimuli such as cold stress, is also down-regulated by YCDs treatment. This suggests that YCDs alleviate cold stress in rapeseed by modulating ROS levels and suppressing MAPK signaling. The down-regulation of plant hormone signal transduction indicates that YCDs treatment does not induce plant hormone accumulation in rapeseed. Furthermore, there is a close relationship between glutathione metabolism and amino acid synthesis, as glutathione synthesis depends on specific amino acids. The down-regulation of glutathione metabolism may promote the accumulation of related amino acids. The relief of cold stress in rapeseed is attributed to the up-regulation of amino acid metabolism pathway genes and other related pathways due to YCDs treatment.

It worth noting that metabolomics data revealed an up-regulation of several amino acids with YCDs treatment. KEGG pathway analysis further identified up-regulation in pathways such as fatty acid elongation, tryptophan metabolism, and biosynthesis of unsaturated fatty acids, which are essential for root growth. Conversely, YCDs treatment led to a down-regulation of starch and sucrose metabolism, as well as nitrogen metabolism, indicating a shift from primary metabolism associated with growth to secondary metabolism, including various amino acid biosynthesis pathways. Additionally, KEGG analysis showed down-regulation of phenylpropanoid biosynthesis and alpha-linolenic acid metabolism pathways in the leaves of oilseed rape treated with YCDs, which is consistent with metabolomics data. This transcriptomic analysis provides significant insights into the molecular mechanisms underlying enhanced cold stress resistance.

In addition, several other pathways related to the production of specialized metabolites were also up-regulated after treated with YCDs. These pathways include the biosynthesis of zeatin, brassinosteroids, cutin, suberine, and wax, these compounds form a hydrophobic layer on aerial surfaces, acting as a primary barrier against environmental changes and mitigating abiotic stress. With deeply diffing the intrinsic transcriptome results, this could be verified by the significantly up-regulation of cytochrome P450 monooxygenases (P450s) [35,36], a key gene family involved in the biosynthesis of zeatin and brassinosteroids, and is also responsible for regulating the biosynthesis of cutin, suberine, and wax. Overall, transcriptome profiling indicates that YCDs treatment activates multiple specialized metabolic pathways, which collectively enhance cold stress resistance and promote root proliferation.

Scavenging over-accumulated ROS in oilseed rape by the YCDs CAT-like enzyme. Under cold environment, root proliferation and activity in oilseed rape seedlings decrease, reactive oxidative species (ROS), a typical plant response to various abiotic stresses, could be accumulated, and presented as highly reactive molecules that can harm cell membranes and enzyme systems [37]. YCDs exhibit ROS scavenging properties scripted from their significant CAT-like activity. Therefore, oxidative stress markers, including hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), hydroxyl radicals (·OH), and malondialdehyde (MDA) were investigated in the oilseed rape roots and shoots under cold stress. In Fig. 6
a, YCDs treated roots showed a 54.81 % decrease in H2O2 concentration (0.503 mmol/gprot) compared to CK (0.227 mmol/gprot), and CDs also reduced H2O2, though less effectively (0.308 mmol/gprot). Similar trends were observed in shoots, where YCDs treatment reduced H2O2 by 32.24 %, exceeding the 15.31 % reduction with CDs. Besides, YCDs treatment enhanced ·OH inhibition in roots by 28.73 % (from 4.441 U/mgprot to 3.165 U/mgprot; Fig. 6 b), whereas CDs showed a smaller decrease (3.15 %, 4.301 U/mgprot). Similarly, YCDs outperformed CDs in reducing ·OH in shoots, indicating YCDs bolster oilseed rape antioxidant defenses. YCDs, with CAT-like activity, effectively reduced H2O2 levels, subsequently lowering ·OH levels more than CDs in oilseed rape seedlings under investigations. Similar results were obtained in DAB and NBT staining experiments (Fig. 6 e and 6f) and the fluorescence imaging results (Fig. 6 g and 6h), showing that obvious restore of H2O2 and •O2– level in leaves of oilseed rape with the application of YCDs compared to CDs treatment.

MDA serves as an indicator for cell membrane lipid peroxidation, reflecting the extent of oxidative damage to cells [38]. In Fig. 6 c, treatment with YCDs reduced MDA concentration in roots by 28.57 % (7.143 nmol/g) compared to CK (10.0 nmol/g), demonstrating that YCDs could inhibit lipid peroxidation in oilseed rape under cold stress. Conversely, CDs led to only a slight decrease of 2.43 % (9.857 nmol/g) in MDA concentration, similar results can be observed in shoots.

Moreover, it worth noting that glutathione (GSH) acts as a crucial antioxidant in plants, enhancing crop resistance to adverse conditions [39]. As illustrated in Fig. 6 d, both roots and shoots exhibited increased GSH concentrations. Specifically, in roots, YCDs treatment elevated GSH levels by 87.87 % (0.945 mgGSH/L) compared to CK (0.503 mgGSH/L), while CDs increased it by 31.01 % (0.659 mgGSH/L). These findings indicate that both YCDs and CDs enhance oilseed rape to adapt cold stress through rebalance the level of ROS, while YCDs demonstrating a better superiority than CDs due to the higher enzymatic activity.

Selective photosynthetically activation induced by nanozymes protect crops from cold stress. Based on the preceding findings, a plausible mechanism for resistance to cold stress is presented in Scheme 1 . In overwintering plants such as oilseed, promoting selective root growth during the seedling stage together with the elimination of excessive ROS is highly desirable for protecting plants from cold damage. For us, YCDs nanozymes can convert unwanted ultraviolet solar light into the beneficial yellow light that selectively stimulates the metabolism of root-promoting amino acids and upregulates genes in the amino acid metabolism pathway, exclusively stimulating root growth. Moreover, YCDs nanozymes exhibit excellent CAT like activity, effectively reducing ROS levels into hemostasis in rapeseed cells. Therefore, the synergistic effect of both root prefoliation and ROS scavenging could facilitate the “selective photosynthetic activation” to oilseed rape within a strengthened root and enhanced resistance to abiotic stress. As a result, the various physiological indicators, including the antioxidant enzyme system of CAT, POD, SOD, GSH and VC was highly improved accompanying by decreasing MDA concentrations. This method demonstrates that a nanozyme-based photosynthetically stimulating strategy can sustainably promote crops growth, potentially opening new avenues to increase crop production and ensure food security to face with the unexpected global climate change nowadays.

In this work, we have presented a new type of selective photosynthetically active nanozymes with bright yellow emission under solar irradiation and catalase-like enzyme activity to selectively promote root growth of the oilseed rape under cold damage. It is demonstrated that the supplement yellow light from the YCDs nanozyme could stimulate the up-regulation of amino acids-related genes crucial for root growth, including fatty acid elongation, tryptophan metabolism, and biosynthesis of unsaturated fatty acids. Moreover, activation pathways contributing to against abiotic stresses were also up-regulated, including biosynthesis of zeatin, brassinosteroids, cutin, suberine, and wax. The metabolomics analysis showed that the amino acids relating to root growth can be increased by ∼ 45 % to ∼ 130 % by the YCDs. Due to excellent CAT like enzyme activity, the ROS levels in plant cells were also effectively reduced. As a result, the YCDs nanozyme could selectively enhance root proliferation of the oilseed rape under cold stress, which is superior to the existing plant growth regulators. The proposed nanozyme-based selective photosynthetically stimulation strategy to enhance root proliferation under abiotic stresses would open a new avenue to enable future crops production, which may provide a sustainable solution for food insecurity issues induced by climate change.

Chemical materials. Chemicals used in this study included o-phenylenediamine (AR, 98 %, Aladdin), H2O2 (30 %, anhydrous, Aladdin), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, >98 %, Aladdin), Hopebio's reagent (solid, Hopebio), DAB solution (1.0 mg/mL, pH = 3.8. Coolaber), C2H5OH (99.5 %, Safe Dry, Adamas). Catalase (CAT), superoxide dismutase (SOD), peroxidase (POD), polyphenol oxidase (PPO), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), malondialdehyde (MDA), hydroxyl radicals (·OH), glutathione (GSH) kit, ascorbic acid (ASA), soluble proteins (SP), total phenolics (TP), chlorophyll and soluble sugars (SS) were purchased from the Nanjing Institute of Biotechnology, China. All reagents, samples and standards were prepared according to the manufacturer's instructions, the calculation of results was performed according to the instructions provided in the test kits, and measurements of each indicator were performed in triplicate, all reagents and solvents were used as is without further purification, and all aqueous solutions were prepared using ultrapure water, unless otherwise stated.

Preparation of YCDs nanozyme and CDs nanomaterials. Briefly, 432 mg of o-phenylenediamine and 60 mL of deionized water were added to the autoclave, which was completely dissolved by ultrasonication, and the tightened autoclave was put into an oven to be warmed up to 160 °C after a certain time and kept for 5 h to end the procedure, with a heating rate of 5 °C/min, and then centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 30 min after cooling, and then filtered by a 0.22 μm pore size filter membrane to obtain the liquid. The liquid was obtained from the carbon fragments, and the solid sample was collected after drying at 60℃, and the powdered YCDs could be obtained after grinding with an agate mortar. For the CDs preparation, briefly, 1.0 g of urea and 3.0 g of citric acid were added in 20.0 mL of deionized water and stirred, and after complete dissolution, the liquid was treated with microwave at 450 W for 2.0 min and then filtered with 0.22 μm nanofiltration membrane and evaporated in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain the CDs nanomaterials.

Characterization of YCDs nanozyme. Various measurements and analyses of YCDs were carried out in this study. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was measured by an X'pert PAN analytical diffractometer at Cu Kα radiation, 40 kV and 40 mA. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was carried out under vacuum conditions using a Thermo Nicolet iS50 FT-IR spectrometer. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were obtained using a JEM-2010 UHR microscope. Fluorescence emission spectroscopy was performed on an LS55.

Study of enzyme-like activity for YCDs. The CAT activity of YCDs was assessed using a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer. Specifically, YCDs (final working concentration: 300 μg mL−1) were injected into PBS buffer (0.1 M, pH = 7.0) containing 30 mM H2O2, and the absorbance change at 282 nm was measured. Kinetic assays were performed in 100 μL of PBS buffer containing 300 μg mL−1 YCDs, 0.5 mM TMB, and different concentrations of H2O2 (0, 18.75, 37.5, 75, 150, 300, and 600 mM). All experiments were repeated three times.

For CAT-like activity, the Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) and the maximum initial velocity (Vmax) was calculated according to:
1 v
=
K M
v max
[S]
+
1
v max
where V represents the initial reaction velocity, Km represents the Michaelis constant, [S] is the concentration of substrate, and Vmax is the maximal reaction velocity.

Experiment of oilseed rape growth. Common kale-type overwintering rapeseed seeds were selected for the experiment. Selected full-grained rape seeds, weighed 26 portions of 1.0 g of rape seeds respectively, added 0.6 ml of seed dressing solution with different concentrations of different materials to each portion, and mixed it with the rape seeds sufficiently and then dried them. Three parallel samples were set up for each treatment, and 30 seeds after the seed mixing treatment were evenly placed in a germination box with 5 × 6 with two pieces of germination paper, supplemented with a certain amount of water, and then put into an incubator with 10℃, 80 % humidity, and 12 h of light and darkness to cultivate for 14 d, and the number of germinations was recorded every day.

The same oilseed rape seeds as in the germination box experiment were selected for potting experiments with vermiculite as the base, and eight treatments of blank, 300 mg/L YCDs is optimal and selected set to carry out the experiment. The eight treatments of different nanomaterials and different concentrations of the same nanomaterials were tested, and 20 potted boxes were sown for each treatment, with 9 rapeseed seeds in each box, and then all the potted plants were placed in a constant-temperature light incubator, with day and night temperatures of 10℃, relative humidity of 75 %∼85 %, and light time of 12 h. The rapeseed was replenished with water to maintain the soil moisture every three days during the growth period, and then harvested after five weeks of growth to determine the various Agronomic data, physiological indexes, and metabolomics and transcriptomics were sent for testing and analysis.

A root scanner (Epson Expression 12000XL) and a universal fiber analyzer (NK-F-02) were selected for further study of the oilseed rape root system. Harvested oilseed rape seedlings were washed clean with pure water several times and then placed in groups of ten into the root scanner for scanning of root morphology, and root length, root tip number, root projection area, and root area were manually exported using Image-J software (National Institutes of Health, Bethesda, MD, USA) to manually derive root length, root tip number, root projection area, and root area. Similarly, the roots of oilseed rape seedlings were washed with ultrapure water and the root tips were made into slides, placed under a microscope and observed with a 10x eyepiece, adjusted until the pictures were clear and then photographed and saved using the software that comes with the system.

Determination of physiological indicators. Oilseed rape seedlings were taken for the determination of physiological indicators. A certain amount of sample leaves (or rhizomes) was cut off with plastic scissors, 1.0 g of leaf (or rhizome) sample was weighed, and the sample was ground in a mortar with liquid nitrogen. The ground sample was put into a 10 ml centrifuge tube, wrapped in tinfoil and stored in an ultra-low temperature refrigerator at −80℃ for testing. Leaf physiological parameters including catalase (CAT), superoxide dismutase (SOD), peroxidase (POD), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), malondialdehyde (MDA), hydroxyl radicals (–OH), glutathione (GSH), ascorbic acid (ASA), soluble proteins (SP), chlorophyll, and soluble sugars (SS) were measured by using the relevant assay kit (Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute, Nanjing, China) for detection. All reagents, samples and standards were configured according to the manufacturer's protocol, and the steps for calculating the results were performed according to the instructions in the kits, with each experiment repeated three times.

DAB and NBT staining was performed according to the following method, leaves and roots of oilseed rape seedlings were stained with 1.0 mg/mL DAB (pH = 3.8) or 0.5 mg/mL NBT at 25 °C for 48 h, and then immersed in 95 % ethanol for 6 h at 70 °C in a water bath to remove chlorophyll. Leaves were recorded by an optical camera and roots by a stereomicroscope ZX-14 (SteREO Discovery V.8).

Confocal imaging of YCDs in oilseed rape. Leaf or root samples were prepared at 27 days of oilseed rape seedling growth. The leaves were then placed on glass slides. A drop of perfluorinated cup (PFD) was placed on each slide to improve the quality of confocal imaging. A coverslip was used to cover the slide to exclude air bubbles. The slides were then placed on a microscope and imaged using a Carl Zeiss laser scanning confocal microscope (LSM880, Germany). Imaging settings: 514 nm YCDs fluorescence emission; 680 nm chloroplast fluorescence emission.

Metabolomics. 25 mg of sample was taken, mixed with 500 μL of extraction solution (MeOH: H2O = 3:1 (v/v)), the extraction solution contains deuterated internal standards. the mixed solution was vortexed for 30 s, sonicated for 10 min in ice water bath, and incubated for 1 h at −40 ℃ to precipitate proteins. Then the samples were centrifuged at 12000 rpm (RCF = 13800 (×g), R = 8.6 cm) for 15 min at 4 ℃. The supernatant was transferred to a fresh glass vial for analysis. LC-MS analyses were performed using an UHPLC system (Vanquish, Thermo Fisher Scientific) with a Waters ACQUITY UPLC BEH Amide (2.1 mm × 50 mm, 2.6 μm) coupled to Orbitrap Exploris 120 mass spectrometer (Orbitrap MS, Thermo). The mobile phase consisted of 0.01 % acetic acid (A) and isopropanol: acetonitrile = 1:1 (v/v) (B). The auto-sampler temperature was 4℃, and the injection volume was 2 μL. The raw data were converted to the mzXML format using ProteoWizard and processed with an in-house program, which was developed using R and based on XCMS, for peak detection, extraction, alignment, and integration. Then an in MS2 database was applied in metabolite annotation. The cutoff for annotation was set at 0.3.

Transcriptomics. Samples were collected from 27 days stress with YCDs treatment or unstress (control) plants were thoroughly washed and flash frozen in liquid nitrogen, then ground into fine powder using pestle and motor. Total RNA from rice leaves was extracted using the TRIzol Regent (Invitrogen CA, USA) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. The quality of the extracted RNA samples was evaluated using an Agilent 2100 Bioanalyzer 2100 (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA). RNA-seq libraries were prepared using the VAHTS Universal V6 RNA-seq library prep kit. The transcriptome sequencing and analysis were conducted using the Illumina Nova seq6000 platform (Illumina), and three biological replicates for each treatment were sequenced. Genes with an adjusted P-value < 0.05 found by DESeq were assigned as differentially expressed. Based on the hypergeometric distribution, GO and KEGG pathway analysis of the DEGs were determined to screen the significant enriched term. The column, chord, and bubble diagrams of the significant enrichment term were drawn with R (v 3.2.0).

Statistical Analysis. Data were analyzed using mean ± standard deviation using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and Duncan's multiple range test, with P < 0.05 as the level of significance of difference. Each experimental treatment was repeated at least three times.

Zixuan Ding: Data curation. Wenlong Xu: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Yingchen Feng: Data curation. Yuan Li: Methodology. Xiaochi Ma: Methodology. Zhenhao Zhou: Investigation. Ke Chen: Investigation. Anis Ali Shah: Methodology. Qianqian Wang: Investigation. Xiaoqian Ren: Investigation. Jinhua Liang: Methodology. Yanan Huang: Methodology. Qingbo Xiao: Methodology. Zhiyang Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (42407034, 22078136), Jiangsu Agricultural Science and Technology Innovation Fund (CX(24)3107), Jiangsu Provincial Natural Science Foundation (BK20241164). The authors would like to thank Weiwei Wang from Shi yan jia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2025.160428.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Using exogenous melatonin, glutathione, proline, and glycine betaine treatments to combat abiotic stresses in crops

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Khalid, M.; Rehman, H. M.; Ahmed, N., et al., Using exogenous melatonin, glutathione, proline, and glycine betaine treatments to combat abiotic stresses in crops. International Journal of Molecular Sciences 2022, 23 (21), 12913.
